topic: eczema flare — trying a new cream

turns.content: my skin is being so dramatic rn i literally cannot
Oh no, what's going on with your skin? Is it a breakout, irritation, something else?

eczema flare... its all up my inner elbows and behind my knees and i look like a reptile shedding lol. i started this new cream from the chemist and its meant to be like ceramide-based and super gentle but honestly it just sits on top of my skin and does nothing??

Ugh, that sounds really uncomfortable. Eczema flares in those spots are the worst because they're in constant contact with movement. With ceramide creams, if it's sitting on top and not absorbing, the base might be too occlusive for your skin type. Some ceramide creams are designed to be thick barrier creams rather than moisturizers. Have you been using anything else alongside it, or is this the only thing you're trying right now?

i have a steroid cream from my gp but i hate using it because it thins your skin over time and thats literally terrifying... so i was trying to manage with just the ceramide one but clearly that is not working. the backs of my knees are so itchy i want to scream

I totally get the concern about steroid creams -- skin thinning is a real side effect with prolonged use. But for short bursts during a bad flare, they're generally considered safe and that's kind of what they're designed for. The key is using them to knock down the inflammation and then switching to your ceramide cream for maintenance. Have you tried applying the ceramide cream on slightly damp skin, like right after a shower? That can make a huge difference with absorption.

wait no i have literally been applying it to dry skin like an idiot... that might actually be the problem. ok i'll try the damp skin thing. also i reckon i should probably just give in and use the steroid for a few days because right now im just suffering for no reason and scratching in my sleep which makes it worse

Yeah exactly -- scratching in your sleep creates that itch-scratch cycle where you damage the skin barrier overnight and then it's worse the next day. Using the steroid for a few days to break that cycle is way better than just toughing it out. A short course to get things under control and then switching to the ceramide cream for ongoing maintenance is a pretty standard approach.

okay real talk though... what do people actually do about the nighttime scratching? because i wake up and my sheets look like a crime scene of dead skin and i have scratch marks everywhere. genuinely grim

A few things that help with the nighttime scratching: keeping your nails really short so you do less damage even if you do scratch, wearing light cotton gloves to bed during bad flares, and keeping the room cool because heat makes itching worse. Some people also find that applying a thicker layer of moisturiser right before bed and covering the worst patches with light cotton bandages helps -- it creates a physical barrier between your nails and the skin.

cotton gloves to bed... i would look absolutely unhinged but honestly at this point i dont care. the crime scene sheets are worse. also the cool room thing makes sense because i have been sleeping with a heater on because its winter and that is probably... not helping

The heater is almost certainly making it worse. Dry heated air is really bad for eczema -- it pulls moisture out of your skin while you sleep. If you can, try an extra blanket instead of the heater, or at least point it away from the bed. A small humidifier in the bedroom can also help counteract the dryness if you do need some heat on.

a humidifier... thats actually smart. my housemate has one she uses for her plants that she might let me borrow. okay so game plan is: steroid cream for a week to calm things down, damp skin ceramide application after that, cotton gloves for nighttime, ditch the heater, borrow the humidifier. did i miss anything

That's a solid plan. One more thing -- check your sheets and pillowcases. If they're a polyester or poly-blend fabric, switching to cotton can make a noticeable difference. Synthetic fabrics can trap heat and irritate eczema patches. Even a basic cotton set from Target or Big W would work.

oh no i think my current ones are some poly blend nightmare from kmart... that explains a lot actually. okay adding cotton sheets to the list. genuinely thank you for not just saying 'have you tried not scratching' because if one more person says that to me i will commit a crime lol bye

Ha, "just stop scratching" is up there with "have you tried drinking more water" in the unhelpful advice hall of fame. Your plan is solid -- tackle the flare, fix the application technique, sort out the sleep environment. Hope your skin chills out soon. Take care!
